subtopic: Timing and dosing of Rh prophylaxis
clinicalStem: A 28-year-old Rh-negative woman, G2P1, presents at 28 weeks gestation for routine antenatal care. Her first pregnancy was uneventful and she received appropriate prophylaxis. Her current partner is Rh-positive. Indirect Coombs test (antibody screen) is negative today. She has no vaginal bleeding and fetal movements are normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate management at this visit?
options: No action required; repeat antibody screen at 36 weeks only
Administer 500 IU anti-D immunoglobulin intramuscularly now
Order a Kleihauer-Betke test to guide anti-D dosing
Perform fetal blood sampling to determine fetal Rh status before any prophylaxis
Administer anti-D only if a sensitising event occurs before delivery
explanation: Routine antenatal anti-D prophylaxis (RAADP) is recommended for all non-sensitised Rh-negative women at 28 weeks gestation (and optionally again at 34 weeks with a two-dose regimen, or as a single 1500 IU dose). A dose of 500 IU IM at 28 weeks is standard practice in many guidelines to prevent sensitisation from occult fetomaternal haemorrhage in the third trimester. The negative antibody screen confirms she is not yet sensitised, making prophylaxis appropriate and effective.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Waiting until 36 weeks to recheck without giving prophylaxis at 28 weeks would leave a window of vulnerability during which occult fetomaternal haemorrhage could cause sensitisation.
This is the correct answer: 500 IU anti-D IM at 28 weeks is the standard RAADP dose in the UK/WHO guidelines for non-sensitised Rh-negative women.
The Kleihauer-Betke test is used after a sensitising event or delivery to quantify fetomaternal haemorrhage and adjust anti-D dose; it is not used for routine 28-week prophylaxis.
Fetal blood sampling is an invasive procedure not indicated for routine prophylaxis; fetal Rh typing from cell-free fetal DNA is used in some centres but does not replace routine prophylaxis.
Waiting for a sensitising event is the older, inadequate approach; RAADP addresses the risk of occult haemorrhage in the absence of any identifiable event.
clinicalPearl: All non-sensitised Rh-negative pregnant women should receive routine anti-D prophylaxis at 28 weeks regardless of the absence of sensitising events.
keyClue: Rh-negative, non-sensitised (negative Coombs), 28 weeks gestation — routine prophylaxis is due.
commonExamTrap: Assuming anti-D is only needed after a sensitising event (e.g., bleeding, trauma); the RAADP programme exists precisely because occult haemorrhage occurs without identifiable triggers.
missedConcept: The distinction between routine antenatal prophylaxis and event-triggered prophylaxis, and that RAADP is given at 28 weeks regardless of identified sensitising events.

clinicalStem: A 24-year-old Rh-negative primigravida at 10 weeks gestation presents to the emergency department after a confirmed complete miscarriage. Evacuation of retained products of conception (ERPC) was performed under general anaesthesia. Her blood group is O Rh-negative. Her antibody screen taken on admission was negative. What is the correct next step regarding Rh prophylaxis?
options: No anti-D is required as the pregnancy was less than 12 weeks and the miscarriage was complete
Administer 250 IU anti-D IM within 72 hours of the ERPC
Repeat the antibody screen in 6 weeks before deciding on anti-D
Administer 500 IU anti-D IM only if the father is confirmed Rh-positive
Anti-D is not required after miscarriage, only after live births
explanation: Any surgical intervention for pregnancy loss (including ERPC), regardless of gestational age, is a sensitising event that mandates anti-D prophylaxis in Rh-negative unsensitised women. For sensitising events at less than 12 weeks, a dose of 250 IU anti-D IM is recommended and must be given within 72 hours. The father's Rh status is not required before administration; anti-D should not be withheld pending paternity or fetal Rh typing.
wrongAnswerExplanations: While some guidelines suggest anti-D may be omitted for spontaneous complete miscarriage before 12 weeks without surgical intervention, an ERPC is a surgical sensitising event that mandates prophylaxis regardless of gestation.
This is correct: 250 IU anti-D IM within 72 hours is the appropriate dose for a sensitising event (including ERPC) at under 12 weeks.
Delaying to repeat the antibody screen before administering anti-D is incorrect; prophylaxis must be given within 72 hours of the sensitising event to be effective.
Anti-D should not be withheld pending confirmation of the father's Rh status — the default is to protect the mother.
Anti-D is required after any sensitising event including miscarriage, ectopic pregnancy, and invasive procedures, not only live births.
clinicalPearl: Surgical intervention for miscarriage (ERPC) is always a sensitising event requiring anti-D prophylaxis, even before 12 weeks — 250 IU IM within 72 hours.
keyClue: Rh-negative + ERPC (surgical sensitising event) = anti-D within 72 hours mandatory.
commonExamTrap: Thinking anti-D is not needed before 12 weeks; this rule applies only to threatened miscarriage without intervention — not to surgical procedures.
missedConcept: Surgical uterine instrumentation is a sensitising event at any gestation, mandating anti-D prophylaxis regardless of how early the pregnancy is.

topic: Haemolytic disease of the newborn — monitoring
subtopic: Screening and surveillance in alloimmunised pregnancy
clinicalStem: A 32-year-old Rh-negative woman, G3P2, is referred at 14 weeks gestation. Her antibody screen shows anti-D titre of 1:32. Her previous second child had neonatal jaundice requiring phototherapy. Current ultrasound shows a singleton pregnancy. She asks what monitoring her baby will require for haemolytic disease of the newborn (HDN). Which investigation is the most appropriate next step to assess severity of fetal anaemia?
options: Weekly serial middle cerebral artery peak systolic velocity (MCA-PSV) Doppler measurements
Amniocentesis for bilirubin optical density (OD450) measurement
Cordocentesis for fetal haematocrit immediately
Serial maternal anti-D titres every 4 weeks
Fetal scalp blood sampling at 34 weeks
explanation: Middle cerebral artery peak systolic velocity (MCA-PSV) Doppler is now the standard non-invasive method to detect fetal anaemia in alloimmunised pregnancies. A MCA-PSV greater than 1.5 multiples of the median (MoM) for gestational age indicates significant fetal anaemia and should prompt further assessment or intrauterine transfusion. It has replaced amniocentesis for OD450 as the primary surveillance tool because it is non-invasive and highly accurate for predicting moderate-to-severe anaemia.
wrongAnswerExplanations: MCA-PSV Doppler is the correct first-line non-invasive monitoring tool and has replaced amniocentesis in modern practice.
Amniocentesis for OD450 (Liley curve) is the older method now largely replaced by MCA-PSV Doppler; it carries procedural risk including worsening fetomaternal haemorrhage.
Cordocentesis is invasive, carries a fetal loss risk of 1-2%, and is reserved for cases where MCA-PSV indicates severe anaemia requiring intrauterine transfusion — not as a first-step investigation.

Serial maternal titres are useful early in pregnancy (titres below 1:16 are generally reassuring) but are unreliable predictors of fetal anaemia severity once the titre exceeds a critical threshold (typically 1:16–1:32).
Fetal scalp blood sampling is an intrapartum procedure and has no role in antenatal surveillance of fetal anaemia.
clinicalPearl: MCA-PSV Doppler > 1.5 MoM is the non-invasive gold standard for detecting fetal anaemia in Rh-alloimmunised pregnancies.
keyClue: Established anti-D sensitisation (titre 1:32) with prior affected infant — non-invasive surveillance for fetal anaemia is the priority.
commonExamTrap: Choosing amniocentesis for OD450, which is the older technique now superseded by MCA-PSV Doppler for fetal anaemia surveillance.
missedConcept: MCA-PSV Doppler has replaced amniocentesis as the primary tool for fetal anaemia surveillance in alloimmunised pregnancies.

sourceLecture: Thrombocytopenia and bleeding _disorder in the pregnancy
sourceFile: Thrombocytopenia and bleeding _disorder in the pregnancy.pdf
clinicalStem: A 30-year-old woman, G1P0, at 32 weeks gestation attends her routine antenatal visit. Full blood count shows platelets of 98 × 10⁹/L. She is asymptomatic with no petechiae, bruising, or mucosal bleeding. Blood pressure is 116/74 mmHg. Urine dipstick is negative for protein. LFTs and renal function are normal. She has no prior history of thrombocytopenia outside of pregnancy. Which diagnosis best explains her presentation?
options: Immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP)
HELLP syndrome
Gestational thrombocytopenia
Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP)
Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC)
explanation: Gestational thrombocytopenia accounts for approximately 70–80% of thrombocytopenia in pregnancy. It is characterised by mild-to-moderate thrombocytopenia (platelets typically 70–150 × 10⁹/L), occurs in the second or third trimester, is asymptomatic, and resolves spontaneously after delivery. The absence of hypertension, proteinuria, or abnormal LFTs excludes HELLP syndrome, and the absence of prior thrombocytopenia outside pregnancy and lack of systemic features makes ITP less likely.
wrongAnswerExplanations: ITP can occur in pregnancy but typically presents with lower platelet counts, may have a history of thrombocytopenia outside of pregnancy, and may have positive antiplatelet antibodies; it is less likely here.
HELLP syndrome requires hypertension, elevated liver enzymes, and low platelets as a triad; the normal BP and LFTs exclude this diagnosis.
Gestational thrombocytopenia is the most common cause of thrombocytopenia in pregnancy and fits all the clinical features here — this is the correct answer.
TTP presents with microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia, neurological symptoms, renal impairment, and fever; the clinical picture here does not fit TTP.

DIC is associated with an underlying trigger (sepsis, abruption, pre-eclampsia), abnormal coagulation screen (prolonged PT/APTT, low fibrinogen), and is typically not an incidental finding; this patient is asymptomatic with no known trigger.
clinicalPearl: Gestational thrombocytopenia is the most common cause of thrombocytopenia in pregnancy: mild, asymptomatic, no hypertension/proteinuria, resolves postpartum — no treatment needed.
keyClue: Asymptomatic, mild thrombocytopenia at 32 weeks with normal BP, normal urinalysis, and no prior history outside of pregnancy.
commonExamTrap: Confusing gestational thrombocytopenia with ITP; in gestational thrombocytopenia, platelets rarely fall below 70 × 10⁹/L and the woman has no prior thrombocytopenia history.
missedConcept: Gestational thrombocytopenia is benign and self-limiting; distinguishing it from ITP and HELLP is a core clinical skill.

sourceLecture: Thrombocytopenia and bleeding _disorder in the pregnancy
sourceFile: Thrombocytopenia and bleeding _disorder in the pregnancy.pdf
subtopic: First-line treatment of ITP in pregnancy
clinicalStem: A 26-year-old woman, G2P1, is seen at 20 weeks gestation. She has a known diagnosis of immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP) diagnosed 2 years ago, managed intermittently with steroids. Her platelet count today is 28 × 10⁹/L. She has no active bleeding, but has mild petechiae on her lower legs. What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological treatment at this stage?
options: Intravenous anti-D immunoglobulin 75 mcg/kg
Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day
Azathioprine 2 mg/kg/day
Rituximab 375 mg/m² IV weekly
Platelet transfusion immediately
explanation: For ITP in pregnancy requiring treatment, oral corticosteroids (prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day) remain the first-line pharmacological option. They are effective, widely available, and the safety profile in pregnancy is well established. Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) is a second-line or adjunct option for refractory cases or urgent pre-delivery platelet rise. Azathioprine and rituximab are not safe in pregnancy. Platelet transfusion is reserved for active bleeding or pre-procedural cover.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Anti-D immunoglobulin can be used for ITP in non-pregnant Rh-positive patients but is not a standard first-line treatment in pregnancy for ITP; this option confuses Rh prophylaxis with ITP therapy.
Oral prednisolone is correct: it is the first-line treatment for ITP in pregnancy when platelet counts require intervention.
Azathioprine is a teratogen (FDA category D) and is contraindicated in pregnancy, particularly in the first and second trimesters.
Rituximab (anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody) is contraindicated in pregnancy due to neonatal B-cell depletion and infection risk.

Platelet transfusion provides only a transient rise (hours to days), does not address the underlying autoimmune process, and is reserved for emergency bleeding episodes or pre-operative/delivery cover — not routine management of mild petechiae.
clinicalPearl: First-line treatment for ITP in pregnancy is oral prednisolone; IVIG is reserved for refractory disease or urgent pre-delivery platelet increase.
keyClue: ITP with platelets 28 × 10⁹/L, petechiae, 20 weeks pregnant — pharmacological treatment needed; prednisolone is first-line.
commonExamTrap: Choosing platelet transfusion as definitive management; transfusions are temporary and do not treat the underlying immune-mediated destruction.
missedConcept: Azathioprine and rituximab are contraindicated in pregnancy; only corticosteroids and IVIG are safe pharmacological options for ITP in pregnancy.

sourceLecture: Thrombocytopenia and bleeding _disorder in the pregnancy
sourceFile: Thrombocytopenia and bleeding _disorder in the pregnancy.pdf
clinicalStem: A 35-year-old woman, G1P0, with known type 1 von Willebrand disease (vWD) presents at 34 weeks gestation. She reports a lifelong history of easy bruising and menorrhagia. Her current vWF:Ag level is 95 IU/dL (within normal range for third trimester). She asks about the risk of bleeding at delivery and what preventive measures should be taken. Which statement best reflects the correct peripartum plan?
options: No special precautions are needed as vWF levels are now normal in the third trimester
Desmopressin (DDAVP) should be given at onset of labour regardless of current vWF levels
vWF levels and factor VIII should be checked at 34–36 weeks and again postpartum; DDAVP or factor concentrate should be available for use if levels fall postpartum
Tranexamic acid should be started at 28 weeks to prevent all peripartum haemorrhage
Elective caesarean section is mandatory in all women with vWD to prevent bleeding
explanation: In type 1 vWD, vWF levels typically rise during pregnancy (due to oestrogen and increased synthesis) and may reach normal range by the third trimester, creating a false reassurance. However, vWF levels fall sharply postpartum to pre-pregnancy levels, substantially increasing the risk of secondary postpartum haemorrhage. Guidelines recommend checking levels at 34–36 weeks to confirm adequacy for delivery, and again postpartum. DDAVP or vWF/factor VIII concentrate should be available for use if postpartum levels are inadequate. Routine prophylactic DDAVP at onset of labour regardless of levels is not indicated if levels are already sufficient.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Normal vWF levels in the third trimester are reassuring for delivery but postpartum levels drop acutely and the patient remains at risk for secondary PPH; monitoring is still required.
DDAVP given at onset of labour regardless of current vWF levels is not standard practice; treatment is guided by vWF levels and clinical need, as DDAVP can cause hyponatraemia and fluid retention.
This is correct: check vWF and FVIII at 34–36 weeks and postpartum, with DDAVP or concentrate available for the postpartum period when levels fall.
Tranexamic acid may be used as adjunctive therapy peripartum but is not started prophylactically at 28 weeks as routine practice for vWD.
Caesarean section is not mandated for vWD; vaginal delivery is acceptable with appropriate haematological preparation and support.
clinicalPearl: vWF levels rise during pregnancy in type 1 vWD but fall sharply postpartum — the highest risk period for bleeding is the puerperium, not delivery itself.
keyClue: Type 1 vWD, currently normal vWF in third trimester — remember the postpartum drop and plan accordingly.
commonExamTrap: Assuming that a normal vWF level in the third trimester means no further action is needed; the postpartum drop creates significant secondary PPH risk.
missedConcept: vWF levels normalise physiologically in pregnancy but drop postpartum; peripartum planning must account for both delivery and the puerperium.

clinicalStem: A 29-year-old woman with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is undergoing her first IVF cycle. During ovarian stimulation with gonadotropins, follicle monitoring at day 9 shows 22 follicles across both ovaries with a lead follicle of 18 mm and oestradiol of 6,200 pmol/L. She is at high risk for ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS). Which intervention is most effective at preventing severe OHSS in this setting?
options: Reduce gonadotropin dose by 25% and continue stimulation as planned
Trigger ovulation with hCG 10,000 IU as scheduled
Cancel the cycle entirely and restart with a lower dose next month
Trigger with GnRH agonist and freeze all embryos (freeze-all strategy)
Administer cabergoline 0.5 mg orally daily for 7 days before egg collection
explanation: In patients at high risk for OHSS (especially PCOS with large follicle cohort and high oestradiol), using a GnRH agonist trigger instead of hCG for final oocyte maturation significantly reduces the risk of severe OHSS because it induces an endogenous LH surge that is shorter-lived than exogenous hCG. Combining this with a freeze-all embryo strategy (cryopreserving all embryos and deferring transfer) eliminates the luteal phase hCG from a potential early pregnancy, which is the main driver of late-onset severe OHSS. This approach preserves embryos and avoids the dangerous consequences of severe OHSS.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Reducing the gonadotropin dose at this stage (coast) may lower oestradiol slightly but does not adequately prevent severe OHSS once follicle recruitment is already maximal.
Standard hCG trigger in this high-risk scenario significantly increases the risk of severe OHSS due to hCG's prolonged half-life sustaining luteal phase ovarian stimulation.
Cycle cancellation is an option but is considered a last resort; it prevents OHSS but results in wasted stimulation and patient distress without preserving embryos.
GnRH agonist trigger combined with freeze-all strategy is the most effective evidence-based approach to prevent severe OHSS while preserving embryos — this is the correct answer.

Cabergoline (a dopamine agonist) reduces vascular permeability and can reduce mild-to-moderate OHSS risk but is not as effective as a GnRH agonist trigger plus freeze-all for high-risk cases and does not address the late-onset OHSS risk from early pregnancy.
clinicalPearl: GnRH agonist trigger + freeze-all strategy is the most effective prevention for severe OHSS in high-risk patients, especially those with PCOS.
keyClue: PCOS + 22 follicles + very high oestradiol (6,200 pmol/L) = very high OHSS risk; GnRH agonist trigger and freeze-all is the prevention strategy.
commonExamTrap: Choosing cabergoline as the primary prevention strategy; while it has a role, it does not address the late-onset OHSS risk from early pregnancy hormones and is not as definitive as the GnRH agonist trigger with freeze-all approach.
missedConcept: GnRH agonist trigger provides a shorter-lasting LH surge than hCG, and freeze-all avoids hCG from early pregnancy — together they eliminate both early and late OHSS.

clinicalStem: A 27-year-old woman with irregular cycles (occurring every 45–90 days) is investigated for infertility. She has a BMI of 24 kg/m². Day 2 FSH is 5 IU/L, LH is 10 IU/L, AMH is 6.8 ng/mL, and testosterone is mildly elevated. Pelvic ultrasound confirms polycystic ovaries. Her tubes are patent on hysterosalpingography and her partner's semen analysis is normal. She is started on clomifene citrate for ovulation induction. Which best describes the mechanism by which clomifene citrate induces ovulation?
options: It acts as a GnRH agonist, directly stimulating FSH and LH secretion from the pituitary
It blocks oestrogen receptors in the hypothalamus, removing negative feedback and increasing GnRH and gonadotropin secretion
It directly stimulates the ovarian follicles to produce oestradiol
It suppresses prolactin levels, thereby allowing normal follicular development
It acts as a selective progesterone receptor modulator to induce endometrial shedding and reset the cycle
explanation: Clomifene citrate is a selective oestrogen receptor modulator (SERM) that competitively binds to oestrogen receptors in the hypothalamus, blocking the normal negative feedback of endogenous oestrogen. This leads to increased pulsatile GnRH secretion, which in turn stimulates the pituitary to release more FSH and LH, driving follicular development and ovulation. It does not directly stimulate the ovary or pituitary.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Clomifene is not a GnRH agonist; GnRH agonists (e.g., buserelin, leuprolide) work differently by initially stimulating then downregulating the pituitary. Clomifene acts at the hypothalamus by blocking oestrogen receptors.
This is the correct mechanism: clomifene blocks hypothalamic oestrogen receptors, reducing negative feedback and increasing GnRH/FSH/LH secretion.
Clomifene does not directly stimulate ovarian follicles; it works upstream at the hypothalamo-pituitary axis.
Clomifene does not suppress prolactin; dopamine agonists (e.g., cabergoline, bromocriptine) are used for hyperprolactinaemia.
Clomifene is a SERM but its therapeutic role in ovulation induction is via the hypothalamus, not through progesterone receptor modulation or endometrial shedding.
clinicalPearl: Clomifene citrate induces ovulation by blocking hypothalamic oestrogen receptors, removing negative feedback and increasing FSH/LH secretion.
keyClue: PCOS with anovulation, normal BMI — clomifene is first-line; its mechanism is anti-oestrogenic at the hypothalamus.
commonExamTrap: Confusing clomifene's mechanism with direct pituitary or ovarian stimulation; it works at the hypothalamic level by blocking oestrogen feedback.
missedConcept: Clomifene's SERM action at the hypothalamus distinguishes it from gonadotropins, which directly stimulate the ovary.

clinicalStem: A 33-year-old woman undergoes a frozen embryo transfer (FET) in a hormone replacement therapy (HRT) cycle. Exogenous oestradiol was used to prepare the endometrium. A single blastocyst is transferred on day 5 of progesterone supplementation. She asks her clinician why she needs to continue vaginal progesterone pessaries after the embryo transfer. Which of the following best explains the rationale for progesterone supplementation in this setting?
options: To stimulate endogenous LH production and maintain the corpus luteum
To replace the progesterone that would normally be produced by the corpus luteum, as no ovulation occurred in an HRT-cycle FET
To prevent hyperstimulation syndrome by reducing FSH levels
To thicken the cervical mucus and prevent ascending infection after transfer
To induce decidualisation of the myometrium to support implantation
explanation: In a hormone replacement therapy (HRT) frozen embryo transfer cycle, no ovulation occurs — the follicle is suppressed and no corpus luteum is formed. Therefore, there is no endogenous source of progesterone. Exogenous progesterone supplementation (typically via vaginal pessaries) is essential to create the progestogenic endometrial environment required for implantation and early pregnancy maintenance. The supplementation must continue until the placenta assumes adequate steroidogenesis, typically around 10–12 weeks of gestation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: In an HRT-FET cycle, there is no corpus luteum to maintain because ovulation was suppressed; luteal phase support is about replacing, not stimulating, progesterone production.
This is the correct rationale: in a HRT-FET cycle, the corpus luteum is absent and exogenous progesterone replaces its function for implantation and early pregnancy support.
Progesterone does not prevent OHSS; OHSS prevention relates to gonadotropin and trigger protocols.
While progesterone does affect cervical mucus, this is not the primary reason for luteal phase support after embryo transfer.
Decidualisation involves the endometrial stroma (not myometrium); while progesterone does drive endometrial decidualisation, calling it myometrial and framing it as the sole rationale is inaccurate.
clinicalPearl: In HRT-FET cycles, no corpus luteum is formed; exogenous progesterone is mandatory for implantation and must continue until placental steroidogenesis is established (~10–12 weeks).
keyClue: HRT-cycle FET = no corpus luteum = no endogenous progesterone = exogenous replacement is essential.
commonExamTrap: Assuming a corpus luteum is always present after IVF; in HRT-FET cycles specifically, the cycle is anovulatory and no corpus luteum forms.
missedConcept: The corpus luteum is the source of progesterone in natural cycles; HRT-FET cycles bypass ovulation and require full exogenous progesterone support.

clinicalStem: A 40-year-old woman presents with heavy menstrual bleeding and bulk symptoms including urinary frequency. Pelvic MRI confirms multiple intramural fibroids, the largest measuring 8 cm. Her haemoglobin is 8.5 g/dL. She wishes to preserve fertility. The gynaecologist plans a myomectomy in 3 months and prescribes a GnRH agonist (leuprolide depot). Which is the PRIMARY reason for using GnRH agonist therapy prior to myomectomy in this patient?
options: To permanently eliminate fibroids and avoid the need for surgery
To reduce fibroid volume and correct anaemia, facilitating a safer surgical procedure
To induce ovulation and improve fertility before surgery
To prevent adhesion formation during myomectomy
To reduce the risk of malignant transformation before surgery
explanation: Pre-operative GnRH agonist therapy (typically for 3 months) prior to myomectomy is used to reduce fibroid volume (by up to 30–50%) through hypo-oestrogenic suppression, reduce intraoperative blood loss, and allow correction of anaemia. This makes myomectomy technically easier, may allow a smaller incision or laparoscopic approach, and reduces transfusion requirements. GnRH agonists do not eliminate fibroids permanently — fibroids regrow after cessation of therapy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: GnRH agonists reduce fibroid size temporarily; fibroids regrow after stopping treatment, so they are not a permanent cure.
This is the correct primary rationale: fibroid volume reduction and anaemia correction before surgery to optimise surgical safety and outcomes.
GnRH agonists suppress ovulation and the gonadal axis; they do not improve fertility and would not be prescribed to induce ovulation.
GnRH agonists do not prevent adhesions; adhesion prevention during myomectomy relates to surgical technique and anti-adhesion barriers (e.g., Interceed).
Fibroids are not pre-malignant lesions; leiomyosarcoma arises de novo and not from existing fibroids, so malignant transformation is not a concern driving pre-operative GnRH use.
clinicalPearl: Pre-operative GnRH agonists before myomectomy shrink fibroids by up to 50% and correct anaemia — effects are temporary, but they optimise surgical conditions.
keyClue: Large fibroids (8 cm), Hb 8.5 g/dL, surgery planned in 3 months — pre-operative GnRH agonist corrects anaemia and reduces fibroid size.
commonExamTrap: Thinking GnRH agonists are a permanent treatment; they are pre-operative adjuncts only, as fibroids regrow once therapy stops.
missedConcept: GnRH agonists work pre-operatively by inducing hypo-oestrogenism to temporarily shrink fibroids and reverse anaemia, not as definitive medical therapy.

topic: Uterine fibroids — screening and incidental finding
clinicalStem: A 38-year-old nulliparous woman attends for a routine well-woman check. She has no gynaecological complaints. A pelvic ultrasound is performed incidentally and reveals a single intramural fibroid measuring 2.5 cm. She has regular, moderate menstrual periods, no pelvic pain, normal urinary function, and her BMI is 22 kg/m². She is not currently planning pregnancy. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Urgent referral to a gynaecologist for myomectomy within 4 weeks
Start oral tranexamic acid immediately to prevent future heavy bleeding
Reassure, advise return if symptoms develop, and offer annual review ultrasound
Start combined oral contraceptive pill to suppress fibroid growth
Perform hysteroscopy to exclude submucosal extension
explanation: Asymptomatic fibroids in a woman with no current symptoms and no immediate fertility plans require no active treatment. The appropriate management is conservative: reassurance, patient education about symptoms to watch for (heavy bleeding, pelvic pain, pressure symptoms), and annual or interval ultrasound surveillance to monitor size. Intervention is reserved for symptomatic fibroids or those causing fertility problems.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Urgent surgical referral for a small asymptomatic fibroid is inappropriate and does not follow evidence-based guidelines; surgery carries risks and is not indicated without symptoms.
There is no active bleeding problem; starting tranexamic acid prophylactically for an asymptomatic patient is not indicated.
Reassurance with surveillance is the correct approach for an asymptomatic small fibroid — this is the correct answer.
No evidence supports the use of the combined pill specifically to suppress fibroid growth; oestrogen-containing preparations may theoretically promote fibroid growth and are not standard prophylaxis.

Hysteroscopy is indicated for suspected submucosal fibroids or unexplained heavy bleeding/infertility; ultrasound here shows an intramural fibroid and the patient is asymptomatic, so hysteroscopy is not indicated.
clinicalPearl: Asymptomatic fibroids in women not planning fertility do not require treatment; annual surveillance ultrasound is appropriate while watchful waiting is maintained.
keyClue: Single small intramural fibroid, completely asymptomatic, not planning pregnancy — conservative management with surveillance is correct.
commonExamTrap: Over-treating an incidental asymptomatic fibroid with surgery or medication; intervention is guided by symptoms or fertility impact, not mere presence.
missedConcept: Not all fibroids need treatment; size and symptom burden determine management, and observation is first-line for asymptomatic cases.

topic: Red degeneration of fibroid in pregnancy
subtopic: Emergency management of acute fibroid degeneration
clinicalStem: A 32-year-old woman, G2P1, at 22 weeks gestation presents to the emergency department with acute onset severe right-sided abdominal pain that began 6 hours ago. She has low-grade fever (37.8°C), localised uterine tenderness over a palpable mass, and a WBC of 14 × 10⁹/L. Fetal heart rate is normal at 154 bpm. Ultrasound shows a 7 cm uterine fibroid with heterogeneous internal echogenicity consistent with red degeneration. She rates the pain 9/10. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Emergency caesarean section to prevent uterine rupture
IV antibiotics for 48 hours to treat ascending infection
Analgesia with NSAIDs or opioids, hydration, and close monitoring
Immediate laparotomy for myomectomy
Uterine artery embolisation as an emergency procedure
explanation: Red (carneous) degeneration of a fibroid is an acute but self-limiting complication most common in the second trimester of pregnancy. It is caused by venous thrombosis and ischaemia within the fibroid as it outgrows its blood supply. Management is conservative: analgesia (NSAIDs are preferred if used before 28 weeks, given with caution; opioids if needed), IV/oral hydration, rest, and close monitoring of the fetus. The condition resolves over days to weeks. Surgery during pregnancy carries significant risks and is not indicated for red degeneration.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Emergency caesarean section is not indicated; the fetus is not in distress and red degeneration does not cause uterine rupture — it is a self-limiting ischaemic process within the fibroid.
The fever and leucocytosis are due to the inflammatory response from fibroid necrosis, not infection; antibiotics are not indicated unless there is clear evidence of ascending infection.
Conservative management with analgesia, hydration, and monitoring is correct; this is a self-limiting condition managed without surgery.
Myomectomy during pregnancy carries high risk of haemorrhage, preterm labour, and fetal loss; it is contraindicated for red degeneration, which is a self-limiting process.
Uterine artery embolisation is contraindicated in pregnancy due to risk of fetal ischaemia and preterm labour.
clinicalPearl: Red degeneration of a fibroid in pregnancy is managed conservatively with analgesia and hydration; surgery is not indicated as it resolves spontaneously.
keyClue: Pregnancy + heterogeneous fibroid + acute localised pain + low-grade fever = red degeneration; conservative management is the answer.
commonExamTrap: Treating as an infection and giving antibiotics; the fever and raised WBC reflect the sterile inflammatory response from fibroid infarction, not bacterial infection.
missedConcept: Red degeneration is an ischaemic, not infectious, complication — conservative analgesia and monitoring are the mainstay of treatment.
redFlagReason: Acute severe abdominal pain in pregnancy with fever and raised WBC must be distinguished from other emergencies (appendicitis, ovarian torsion, placental abruption) before assuming a benign cause; initial stabilisation and monitoring are mandatory.
